parentQuestion: The blood vessels of the skin are one of the few areas of the body where the vessels are innervated by both the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions.

scaffolds.q: Cutaneous (skin) blood vessels control blood flow to the skin surface.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Skin blood flow affects thermoregulation, blushing, and pallor responses.

scaffolds.q: Skin blood vessels can constrict (less flow) or dilate (more flow).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Vasoconstriction = cold, pale skin; vasodilation = warm, flushed skin.

scaffolds.q: Most blood vessels in the body are innervated by sympathetic ONLY.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The parasympathetic generally does NOT innervate blood vessels (except erectile tissue). Vascular tone is sympathetic territory.

scaffolds.q: Skin blood vessels follow this same pattern - sympathetic innervation ONLY.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Cutaneous blood vessels are NOT an exception - they receive sympathetic innervation only, like most other blood vessels.

scaffolds.q: Sympathetic vasoconstriction makes skin pale (cold, fear, blood pressure control).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! High sympathetic activity constricts skin vessels, reducing blood flow and making skin pale and cool.

scaffolds.q: Vasodilation in skin occurs by DECREASING sympathetic activity, not by activating parasympathetic.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! With less sympathetic tone, smooth muscle relaxes and vessels dilate. No parasympathetic involvement needed.

scaffolds.q: Erectile tissue is the main exception - parasympathetic DOES cause vasodilation there.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Erectile tissue in genitals requires parasympathetic-mediated vasodilation for engorgement. But skin blood vessels are different.

scaffolds.q: Skin blood vessels are NOT innervated by parasympathetic - only sympathetic.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The question's statement is false - skin vessels are sympathetic-only, not dual innervated.

scaffolds.q: The statement is FALSE - skin blood vessels receive sympathetic innervation ONLY.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The answer is FALSE. Skin blood vessels are NOT 'one of the few areas' with dual innervation - they're actually sympathetic-only like most blood vessels.
